Everyone's sharing "build an AI agent this weekend" threads.

But most agents die after the demo because they skip 3 boring layers:

1. Memory — without persistent context, your agent forgets everything between sessions
2. Tool auth — connecting to real apps (Gmail, Slack, calendars) is where 80% of the integration pain lives
3. Always-on scheduling — an agent you have to manually trigger isn't an agent, it's a script

The tutorials are finally catching up to this. That's the real signal.

One underrated founder skill: saying what you mean clearly.

When it’s just you, messy thoughts are fine.
Once the team grows to 7–8 people, context syncing becomes real work.

Use voice-to-text from day one — especially when talking to AI.

Grok 4.5’s benchmark results are out. The most noteworthy part isn’t that it ranks fourth, but its cost structure:

• Intelligence Index: $0.31 per task, less than one-third the cost of Opus 4.8
• Coding Agent Index: $2.49 per task, one-fifth the cost of Fable 5
• 60%+ fewer output tokens than Opus 4.8
• Pricing itself is 60% lower than competing products

More importantly, Elon revealed that Grok 4.5 has not yet adopted the C/C++ inference engine optimized specifically for GB300, meaning its speed could at least double.

For developers, this means that in coding agent scenarios, you can now get performance close to GPT-5.5 with Grok 4.5, at only half the cost.

Frontier intelligence is rapidly becoming commoditized. Cost efficiency is the new battleground.

Hi, I'm JUNXI. Over the past few months, I kept noticing that my AI workflow was scattered everywhere:

Open one chat to do research → copy the results → paste into another chat to write code → copy the code into a review chat → paste everything back into a doc.

I wasn't working with AI. I was working as AI's courier.

Checked for Mac Mini availability over the weekend 🤓
The delivery date was still Mar 4th for 94041 CA 🤗

Bought one yesterday and the arrival date is Mar 16th now 🤯

Is this the same for everywhere on earth? Or just Bay Area problem 🫠

AI makes it possible for anyone to own a feature end-to-end.

But it also creates new failure modes:

duplicated efforts

blurred accountability

“everyone ships” but nobody maintains

How are you preventing AI-enabled chaos on your team?

Pre-AI: collaboration = handoffs.
Post-AI: collaboration = ownership.
Pre-AI: “I did my part.”
Post-AI: “I own the outcome.”

Feels like a PhD lab:
you don’t get credit for activity, you get credit for what you own.
What changed most in your workflow?

Hot take: AI is killing functional silos.

The new unit of work isn’t a “team”.
It’s a “fully-owned feature”.
When anyone can ship with AI, the only real constraint becomes:
taste + decisions + ownership.

Do you feel your role boundaries disappearing too?

AI is pushing startup teams toward a “PhD lab” model.

Less “PM vs Eng”.
More “Who owns this problem end-to-end?”
It’s empowering… and also scary:
more autonomy, more accountability.

Is this the future of org design—or a fast path to chaos?

One thing I’ve noticed since AI: team collaboration feels more like a PhD lab. 

Less “dev vs product”, more “who owns what”. With copilots everywhere, individual scope expands—anyone can own a feature, even ship a tiny product end-to-end.

I started my PhD at 20.
The program admits ~20 students a year out of thousands.

Most of my peers had years of research experience.

One was an IOI gold medalist who ranked #2 globally.
He switched research areas and published almost immediately.

Before advisor meetings, my stomach would drop.

I delayed them.
Made excuses.
Sometimes I even hoped I’d get sick.

Not because my advisors were harsh.
They were great.

It was in my head.

I wasn’t afraid of failing.

I was afraid of being exposed.

“They’ll wonder why I’m here.”

So instead of making progress, I protected my ego.

I stayed up until 4am reading novels and playing games.
Woke up at noon.
Cooked long meals to feel productive.

I was neither progressing
nor doing anything that could lead to progress.

The shift came when I realized:

What I produce is not who I am.

If output defines identity,
imperfect work becomes a threat.

And if imperfect work is a threat,
you’ll avoid producing anything at all.

So I started sending imperfect updates.
Half-baked ideas.
Early drafts.

It took years to unlearn.

But this is true in research.
And even more true in startups.

You can’t ship if your ego is on the line.

RT @junxi_song: got to hear Rob Ferguson (ex-CTO Microsoft for Startups, now @FireworksAI) speak last night 

he admitted he barely writes…

A few days ago I sat in on a talk by the CTO of Microsoft for Startups. He quoted investor Robert and it stuck with me:

“Competition means people are interested—that’s great.
But assume the big players will copy you before it happens.
Know the answer to ‘What will they never bother going after?’ and build your moat there.”

Since then I’ve been rewiring every product idea through that lens: celebrate the copycats as proof of demand, then double-down on the niche they’ll never find worth the hassle.
I am planning to hold an Openclaw event—who’s interested?

the AI cost trap nobody warns you about:

$5k/month → add features → $15k/month → competition undercuts you from below

use your credits hard to find the business. then find the model that fits the economics.

don't optimize costs before you find product-market fit

Recently I went to San Francisco to attend the first Women Tech Meetup co-hosted by Stripe and Puzzle—the atmosphere was electric.

It was truly exhilarating to be in the same room with so many accomplished women, discussing AI-driven business models, key metrics, and how to position them to meet the expectations of founders in other fields.
